perturbation.original_text: As used in this Agreement, the term "customer" means any customer of the Company whose identity ARC learned through ARC's ownership of the Company or with whom ARC had business contact during the twelve (12) months immediately prior to the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: As used in this Agreement, the term "customer" means any individual who has expressed interest in the Company's services, regardless of whether they have engaged in a formal business transaction, or with whom ARC had any form of contact.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition of "customer" broadens the scope beyond established business relationships or contact in the 12 months prior to the agreement, potentially conflicting with legal interpretations of who constitutes a customer in non-compete clauses. This overreach could make the non-compete unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California law generally prohibits non-compete agreements. Defining 'customer' too broadly could be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint of trade, violating Section 16600, as it restricts ARC's ability to interact with a wider range of potential clients.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the definition of 'customer' to include anyone who has expressed interest, regardless of a formal business transaction or contact within the prior 12 months. This contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which generally voids contracts restraining someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. The law snippet 1 states 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void'. By broadly defining 'customer', the modified agreement potentially restricts ARC's ability to interact with a wider range of potential clients, thus creating an unreasonable restraint of trade under California law.

perturbation.original_text: For a period of five (5) years from the date hereof, ARC shall not, acting directly or indirectly, (i) have any ownership interest in any competing business, (ii) call on, solicit or have business communications with respect to a competing business with any of the Company's customers or prospective customers for the purpose of obtaining competing business from such customer or prospective customer other than for the benefit of the Company, or (iii) participate in the formation or start-up of a competing business.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of five (5) years from the date hereof, ARC shall not, acting directly or indirectly, (i) have any ownership interest in any business, (ii) call on, solicit or have communications of any kind with any of the Company's customers or prospective customers, or (iii) participate in any business whatsoever.
perturbation.explanation: This alteration broadens the non-compete clause to prohibit ARC from engaging in *any* business or communicating with *any* customer, regardless of competition. This is an overreach that is likely unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in scope and duration. Prohibiting *any* business activity or communication is likely an unreasonable restraint of trade under ORC 1331.02, making the clause unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1331.02 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1331.02 | Prohibition against issuing or owning trust certificates or entering into combination, contract, or agreement. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1331 Monopolies Effective: October 1, 1976 Latest Legislation: House Bill 1358 - 111th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF No person shall issue or own trust certificates, and no person shall enter into a combination, contract, or agreement, the purpose and effect of which is to place the management or control of such combination, or the product or service thereof, in the hands of a trustee with the intent to limit or fix the price or lessen the production or sale of an article or service of commerce, use, or consumption, to prevent, restrict, or diminish the manufacture or output of such article or service, or refuse to buy from, sell to, or trade with any person because such person appears on a blacklist issued by, or is being boycotted by, any foreign corporate or governmental entity. Available Versions of this Section October 1, 1976 – House Bill 1358 - 111th General Assembly [ View October 1, 1976 Version ]
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly broadens the non-compete clause, prohibiting ARC from engaging in *any* business or communicating with *any* customer, regardless of whether it is competitive. This is a clear overreach as explained in the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation confirms this likely creates an unreasonable restraint of trade under Ohio law. While the provided law snippet, Section 1331.02, focuses on trusts and monopolies, it indicates Ohio's concern with agreements that limit trade or commerce. The modified text could be interpreted as such an agreement, especially considering the Perturbation and Law Explanations. The overly broad restriction in the changed text likely violates the requirement that non-compete agreements be reasonable in scope.

perturbation.original_text: ARC has learned trade secrets and confidential information of the ------- Company, including, but not limited to, the software of the Company, marketing, price information, customer lists, identities of customer contact persons, lists of prospective customers, market research, sales systems, marketing programs, budgets, pricing strategy, identity and requirements of national accounts, methods of operating, other trade secrets and confidential information regarding customers and employees of the Company or its customers and other information about the Company's Business that is not readily known to the public and gives the Company an opportunity to obtain an advantage over competitors who do not know such information (collectively, the "Confidential Information").
perturbation.changed_text: ARC has access to certain information of the Company (the "Confidential Information").
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific details that constitute Confidential Information, the term becomes overly broad and vague. This makes it difficult to determine what is actually protected.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) - Specifically, the definition adopted by many states based on the UTSA.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA (adopted in many states, including Ohio) requires that a trade secret be 'information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process.' By removing specific descriptions, the modified text fails to meet the UTSA's definition of a trade secret, making it difficult to enforce confidentiality.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the UTSA's requirements for trade secret protection. The Law Explanation states that the UTSA requires specific descriptions of what constitutes a trade secret. By replacing the detailed list of what constitutes 'Confidential Information' with the general statement 'ARC has access to certain information of the Company (the "Confidential Information").', the changed text fails to meet the UTSA's requirement for specific information, as explained by the LLM, thus rendering the term overly broad and vague and making it difficult to enforce. The changed text fails to adequately define the trade secret, creating a misaligned terminology that could lead to non-compliance and unenforceability, fitting the Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction definition. Since the term is overly broad and vague, it is difficult to enforce. There are no useful scraped snippets.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Ohio.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by principles of equity and fairness, as determined by the parties.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing a specific state's law with 'principles of equity and fairness' introduces ambiguity and contradicts established legal frameworks. This makes the contract potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law Principles - Choice of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Contract law generally requires a clear choice of law for enforceability. The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 allows parties to choose the governing law, but 'principles of equity and fairness' is too vague and not a recognized body of law, potentially rendering the agreement unenforceable due to lack of clear governing legal framework.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'laws of the State of Ohio' to 'principles of equity and fairness' replaces a recognized body of law with a vague standard. The Law Explanation supports that 'principles of equity and fairness' is too vague and not a recognized body of law, potentially rendering the agreement unenforceable due to a lack of a clear governing legal framework. This creates a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction because the contract language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract potentially unenforceable.
